did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper proposes DermCDSM, a clinical decision support model for dermatosis that uses machine learning and deep learning. Specifically, it employs an improved Chameleon Swarm Optimization (ICSO) algorithm for image segmentation, Multi-Strategy Seeker Optimization (MSSO) for feature selection, and Convolutional Deep Spiking Neural Networks (CD-SNN) for skin cancer detection and multi-class classification. The model was validated using the ISIC 2017 dataset.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper proposes a novel clinical decision support model (DermCDSM) using specific algorithms (ICSO, MSSO, CD-SNN) and validates its efficacy through experimental results on the ISIC 2017 dataset, presenting original performance metrics and comparative analysis.

title: DermCDSM: Clinical Decision Support Model for Dermatosis using Systematic Approaches of Machine Learning and Deep Learning
brief_summary: This study proposes DermCDSM, a clinical decision support model for the detection and classification of dermatoses, including skin cancer. The framework integrates an improved Chameleon Swarm Optimization (ICSO) algorithm for image segmentation, Multi-Strategy Seeker Optimization (MSSO) for feature selection, and Convolutional Deep Spiking Neural Networks (CD-SNN) for classification. The model was validated using the benchmark ISIC 2017 dataset, which includes classes such as Eczema, Psoriasis, Vitiligo, Melanoma, Carcinoma, and Blue Nevus. Performance was evaluated by comparing CD-SNN against baseline methods like the ABCDE rule, SVM, and DGC-NB on both original and augmented data. The results demonstrated that the proposed CD-SNN technique achieved superior accuracy, precision, recall, and F-measure compared to existing approaches, confirming its efficacy for automated skin disease diagnosis.

all_task_types.target: Multi-class classification of skin diseases (Eczema, Psoriasis, Vitiligo, Melanoma, Carcinoma, Blue Nevus)
all_task_types.details: The paper implements Convolutional Deep Spiking Neural Networks (CD-SNN) for 'multi-class classification' of dermatoses using the ISIC 2017 dataset. The model predicts discrete diagnostic categories for six specific skin conditions. Performance is evaluated using accuracy, precision, recall, specificity, and F-measure for each class.

The framework includes an improved Chameleon Swarm Optimization (ICSO) algorithm for 'image segmentation' to identify the targeted disease region, which serves as a preprocessing step for the classification task.

datasets_used.name: ISIC 2017
datasets_used.description: The International Skin Imaging Collaboration Challenge 2017 dataset, a publicly available repository of high-resolution dermoscopy images for skin disease classification. It was used in its original form and after data augmentation (rotation, flipping, zooming, brightness/contrast adjustments, shearing).

use.training: Used for training the Convolutional Deep Spiking Neural Network (CD-SNN) model. Original samples were augmented to increase dataset size and mitigate imbalance.
use.testing: Used for evaluating the performance of the CD-SNN model and comparing it against baseline methods (ABCD rule, SVM, DGC-NB).
use.other: Used for segmentation validation via the ICSO algorithm.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance comparisons between the proposed CD-SNN model and baseline methods (ABCD rule, SVM, DGT-NB) were based on descriptive metrics (accuracy, precision, recall, specificity, F-score) and percentage improvements, without statistical significance testing.

data_augmentation_techniques.details: Images were rotated by specific angles such as 90 degrees or 180 degrees to help models become pivot-invariant and distinguish contrasts in the direction of skin sores.
Images were increased or decreased equally or unevenly to simulate various degrees of nearness to a skin sore, helping models perceive injuries at various scales.
Images were reflected along the vertical axis to create variations in the appearance and location of skin sores.
Images were reflected along the horizontal axis to create variations in the appearance and location of skin sores.
Applied as part of image enhancement techniques (contrast change, histogram smoothing) to improve visual qualities and make significant elements and surfaces easier to imagine.
Listed as a data augmentation technique used during training to expand the dataset size and mitigate data imbalance.

conclusions: The proposed DermCDSM framework, which integrates Improved Chameleon Swarm Optimization (ICSO) for segmentation, Multi-Strategy Seeker Optimization (MSSO) for feature selection, and Convolutional Deep Spiking Neural Networks (CD-SNN) for classification, demonstrates superior efficacy compared to existing approaches like the ABCD rule, SVM, and DGC-NB.

The CD-SNN model achieved high performance on the ISIC 2017 dataset, with testing accuracy reaching 96.819% for Melanoma and 95.070% for the augmented dataset overall, significantly outperforming baseline methods in accuracy, precision, recall, and F-measure.

The integration of ICSO and MSSO effectively addresses challenges related to data dimensionality and feature selection, enhancing the overall accuracy and reliability of the disease detection process.

The study validates the model's generalization capability by testing on multiple public datasets, including ISIC 2016, PH2, and ISIC 2017 (both original and augmented), showing consistent high performance across these benchmarks.

The framework provides a transparent and interpretable foundation for skin disease detection through the application of Explainable Artificial Intelligence (XAI), supporting its potential for clinical decision support.
important_remarks: The study relies exclusively on the ISIC 2017 dataset (with some validation on ISIC 2016 and PH2), which may limit generalizability to other populations or imaging devices not represented in these benchmarks.

The paper does not report any statistical significance testing (e.g., p-values) for the performance comparisons between the proposed CD-SNN model and baseline methods (ABCD rule, SVM, DGC-NB); differences are reported as descriptive metrics only.

The 'Carcinoma' class in the ISIC 2017 dataset is used without explicit clarification of whether it refers to Basal Cell Carcinoma (BCC), Squamous Cell Carcinoma (SCC), or a combined category, which could introduce diagnostic ambiguity.

The model was trained and evaluated using data augmentation (rotation, flipping, zooming, brightness/contrast adjustments, shearing) to mitigate class imbalance; however, the paper does not discuss potential overfitting risks associated with this specific augmentation strategy on a relatively small original dataset.

The implementation environment is Google Colab using Python, which may introduce variability in hardware acceleration (GPU/TPU) and reproducibility compared to standardized clinical computing environments.

While the paper claims interpretability via Explainable AI (XAI), it does not provide detailed quantitative metrics for the interpretability or clinical trustworthiness of the explanations generated by the CD-SNN model.

The dataset sizes for certain classes are small (e.g., Psoriasis: 96 training samples; Carcinoma: 110 training samples), which may affect the robustness of the deep learning model for these specific conditions without extensive augmentation.
